title: Training Data vs Test Data
concept: To know if a model actually learned a pattern — instead of just memorizing — you have to check it on examples it has never seen before, called test data, kept separate from the training data it studied.

exercises.prompt: Your friend memorizes the answers to last year's exact history exam, word for word. This year's exam has different (but related) questions. What's the risk?

options.text: They might do poorly, because memorizing old answers isn't the same as understanding the topic
There's no risk — memorized answers always transfer perfectly
The new exam will conveniently repeat the exact same questions

exercises.explain: Right! Memorizing specific answers doesn't guarantee you understand the topic well enough to handle new questions.

exercises.prompt: True or false: The best way to check if a student truly understands a subject is to test them on the exact same questions they studied from.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! Testing on brand-new questions covering the same topic is a much better check for real understanding.

cards.title: Priya studies for a math test
cards.body: Priya's teacher gives her 20 practice problems to study, then promises the real test will use 20 different problems on the same topics. Priya could memorize the exact answers to the practice set — but that won't help on test day. She needs to actually understand the method.

cards.title: Training data vs. test data
cards.body: Training data is the set of examples a model studies to learn a pattern. Test data is a separate set of examples — ones the model has never seen — used afterward to check whether it actually learned the pattern.

cards.title: Practice problems vs. the real exam
cards.body: Training data is like Priya's practice problems — she pores over them to learn the method. Test data is like the real exam: new questions on the same topics, used to check whether she really learned it or just memorized the practice set.

cards.title: Testing the dog-photo model
cards.body: A model studies 8,000 labeled dog and cat photos (training data). To check it, you show it 2,000 brand-new photos it has never seen (test data) and see how many it labels correctly.

cards.title: Grading on the training data doesn't count
cards.body: It's tempting to just check a model's accuracy on the same examples it trained on. That's misleading — a model can score perfectly there just by memorizing answers, without ever learning a pattern that works on new data.

cards.title: Two different jobs
cards.body: Training data and test data serve very different purposes:

right.text:
Held back to check
if it really learned

exercises.prompt: Why do data scientists keep a separate test set instead of grading a model on the same data it trained on?

options.text: Grading on training data can't reveal whether the model truly learned a general pattern or just memorized
Test data is always smaller and cheaper to collect
Training data can only be used one single time
Test data trains the model faster than training data

exercises.explain: Exactly! A model could score 100% on training data purely from memorization — the test set is the honest check.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Test data should include examples the model already saw during training.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! Test data must be examples the model has never seen, or it's not a fair check of what it actually learned.

exercises.sentence: You train a model on training data, then check what it really learned using ___ data.
exercises.explain: Test data is the held-back, unseen set used to honestly evaluate the model.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario correctly uses training data and test data?

options.text: A model studies 5,000 photos, then is graded on 1,000 different photos it never saw
A model studies 5,000 photos, then is graded on those exact same 5,000 photos
A model is graded on photos before it studies any photos at all

exercises.explain: Correct! Studying one set and being graded on a separate, unseen set is exactly what training and test data are for.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A model that scores 99% on training data but only 60% on test data has clearly learned a pattern that generalizes well to new examples.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! That big gap is a warning sign — it suggests the model memorized the training data instead of learning a pattern that works on new examples.

exercises.prompt: A weather-prediction model scores 98% accuracy when tested on the exact days it trained on, but only 55% accuracy on brand-new days it's never seen. What does this most likely tell you?

options.text: The training-data score is misleading; the model may not have learned a pattern that generalizes to new days
The model is excellent and ready to use, since 98% is a great score
Test data scores never matter as long as training scores are high
The weather itself must be broken that day

exercises.explain: Exactly! A huge gap between training and test performance is a red flag that the training score doesn't reflect real-world ability.

exercises.prompt: A student accidentally sees the exact questions for tomorrow's exam today and memorizes the answers. Which best explains why this makes the exam an unfair test of their understanding?

options.text: Their exam score would reflect memorized answers, not their ability to solve new, unseen problems on the topic
It doesn't matter, because studying is studying no matter the source
It makes the exam unfairly harder for them
It proves they understand the topic perfectly

exercises.explain: Right! A fair test — for a student or a model — has to use questions or examples that weren't already seen and memorized.
